metadata_information.title: Equitable provision of long-term public goods: the role of negotiation mandates
metadata_information.version: Python script, from D&R API, generated on 2026-03-19

title_statement.title: Equitable provision of long-term public goods: the role of negotiation mandates

document_description.abstract: In a one-period model, whether or not individual weights in the welfare function are based on initial endowments dictate who provides public goods. But with long-term public goods, banning wealth redistribution still allows for several equilibriums depending on Parties' willingness to acknowledge changes in negotiating powers over time, and on whether or not they care only for their own descendants. Adaptative and universal mandates lead to far more robust equilibrium. In all cases, a simple rule of thumb for allocating expenditures at first period emerges, independent of both the optimal level of public goods and the second-period distribution of expenditures.
